coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical water splitting by a novel design of photo-anode: inverse opal-like UiO-66 sensitized by Pd and decorated with S,N graphene QDs
coredata.dc:creator: Zargazi, Mahboobeh
Entezari, Mohammad H.
coredata.dc:description: UiO-66 film has been interested for photoelectrodes in water splitting, but it has major problems such as inappropriate band gap and resistant behavior under bias potential. An inverse opal structure was synthesized for the first time and sensitized by plasmonic nanoparticles (Pd) and strategically decorated with S,N graphene quantum dots (S,N GQDs). Consequently, the UiO-66/Pd/S,N GQDs photoanode has indicated a remarkable photocurrent density of ≈300 μA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus RHE under AM 1.5G. Applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) is 0.66% at 0.65 V versus RHE and absorbed photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (APCE) is 19.7% under visible light at 500 nm. This high efficiency originated from the engineering of band gap and effective reduction of charge transfer resistance of MOF with the incorporation of Pd nanoparticles. The combination of morphology and composition regulatory of UiO-66 films has key roles in the fabrication of more desirable designing photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical water splitting by a novel design of photo-anode: inverse opal-like UiO-66 sensitized by Pd and decorated with S,N graphene QDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: UiO-66 film has been interested for photoelectrodes in water splitting, but it has major problems such as inappropriate band gap and resistant behavior under bias potential. An inverse opal structure was synthesized for the first time and sensitized by plasmonic nanoparticles (Pd) and strategically decorated with S,N graphene quantum dots (S,N GQDs). Consequently, the UiO-66/Pd/S,N GQDs photoanode has indicated a remarkable photocurrent density of ≈300 μA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus RHE under AM 1.5G. Applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) is 0.66% at 0.65 V versus RHE and absorbed photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (APCE) is 19.7% under visible light at 500 nm. This high efficiency originated from the engineering of band gap and effective reduction of charge transfer resistance of MOF with the incorporation of Pd nanoparticles. The combination of morphology and composition regulatory of UiO-66 films has key roles in the fabrication of more desirable designing photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern world has a special focus on optimized utilization of renewable energies such as sun as a source with high lifetime and available energy for energy crisis [1]. Accordingly, one of the top and cost effective technology is water splitting under sunlight. The two main technologies of water splitting are photocatalyst (PC) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting which both of them use semiconductors as a trapper and absorbent of sun energy [2]. With inspiration from nature, PEC water splitting has been presented as an efficient and cost-effective approach of a man-made cause to photosynthesis [3]. Compared to PC, PEC water splitting has advantages such as separated production of hydrogen and oxygen gases. One of the major and effective components in PEC water splitting are photoelectrodes [4]. Recently, numerous researches developed variety photoanodes including TiO2 [5], Fe2O3 [6], BiVO4 [7], and etc. which produce oxygen gas from PEC water splitting. Special features required for photoanodes are light absorption capability, high surface area and stability in aqueous electrolytes. Recently, our group was focused on the synthesis of visible light active photocatalytic thin films with high efficiency in degradation of pollutants [8–14]. Metal-organic-frameworks (MOFs) are introduced as novel porous materials which inorganic metals by organic ligands joined together to produce complicated structures. MOFs were exhibited several bold features such as permanent porosity with the regulatory ability of pores and ultra high surface area (≈7000 m2/g) compared to other nano-porous materials such as zeolites or inorganic oxides [15].

Herein, it is expected that the MOFs with well-designed active sites should be exciting thin films for electrocatalytic water splitting due to the high density of accessible sites and the presence of adjustable pores. However, most MOFs are electrically insulating at low bias [16], which hinders the charge transport between the active sites in MOFs and strongly limits the practical use of MOFs in photo-electrochemical applications. From another point of view, MOFs can provide a high charge transport rate through their complex structure that is satisfying controversial challenges for MOF-based electrodes. MOFs well done charge transporting in through their skeletons by two routes: redox hopping and band transport [17]. Alessandro et al [18] were discovered the redox hopping feature for Zn-based MOF films which indicated redox-active linkers are responsible for the charge transferring. Furthermore, it should be claimed that MOFs behave like semiconductors due to experimentally obtained calculations of their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). But, MOFs are not real semiconductor like organic semiconductors due to their highly localized electrons and immobility of charge carriers. On the other hand, MOFs can provide long-range pathways for electron transporting through bulk crystal due to their periodically structure. Another prominent problem for MOFs is non-stability in aqueous electrolytes. Zr-based MOF, UiO-66 is semiconducting MOF with higher stability in water electrolyte and has an intrinsic photocatalytic activity in the hydrogen evolution [19]. Until now, different MOF thin films were synthesized such as MIL-53(Fe)[20], NH2-MIL-125 [21] (MOF based nickel-iron), and so on for PEC water splitting but all of them suffering from instability in acidic or basic aqueous electrolytes yet. According to our knowledge, UiO-66 thin films are new and not reported yet. Despite mentioning intrinsic photocatalytic property of UiO-66, it has not any activation in the visible light region. Here, two influential solutions are morphology modification and sensitization. From the morphological point of view, 3-dimentional (3D) ordered structures such as inverse opal was shown highest light harvesting due to several factors, including light multi-scattering, slow photons and band gap manipulation. Recently, a lot of efforts were performed in view of hybrid photoelectrodes to reach high quantum efficiencies [22]. To resolve the inappropriate of band gap and increasing the catalytic active sites behavior of Zr-MOFs, plasmonic metals such as Pt [19], Au [23], Ag [24] or Pd [25] were incorporated in these structures in previous reports. Plasmonic metals due to localized surface plasmonic resonance (LSPR) can produce hot electrons which transferring to the conducting band of MOFs [26]. Herein, Pd despite low-SPR effect compared to Au and Pt, this metal has main advantages such as cheap, available, and used as active catalyst. On the other hand, the LSPR property of Pd can be changed with its size and morphology. It was illustrated that there is a strong effect of LSPR position for Pd size and has a red shift up to 100 nm. Leong et al [27] indicated that Pd nanoparticles with 20-30 nm in size have red shifted from UV position to visible region at around 500 nm. This work indicates that there are some significant advantages in using Pd nanostructures if the size and morphology of the Pd nanoparticles can be controlled to increase both light harvesting and effective surface activity. Recently, there is further interest in Pd nanostructures for photoelectrochemical applications due to its high interaction with light, controllable LSPR, and excellent catalytic activity compared with Ag and Au [23,24,28]. To reach a high efficiency, quantum dots (QDs) are used as a sensitizing agent or as co-catalyst for photocatalysts due to its high surface area and high light absorption ability [29–32]. For instant, UiO-66 nanoparticles were sensitized by CdS [29], CdSe [33], and MoS [34] quantum dots and applied as new design photocatalyts for photocatalytic applications. Modification of MOF with QDs could dissolve the existed problems related to both MOFs (weak light absorption) and QDs (agglomeration and recombination of photogenerated charges) simultaneously [35,36]. Nevertheless, MOF sensitization by QDs is pretended logical solution from view of light absorption. In recent years, nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) are considered due to narrow band gap (≈ 2.5 eV), broadband spectral response, and high carrier transport [37]. Co doping of S and N elements could be broadened light absorption area of graphene quantum dots [38].

Inspired these concepts, for the first time, 3D-inverse opal like UiO-66 films were synthesized using colloidal crystal templates (PS). UiO-66 films systematically decorated with plasmonic metal (Pd) and S, N GQDs. An innovative design of UiO-66/Pd/S, N GQDs was proposed as new high stable photoelectrode in PEC water splitting systems. High resulted photocurrent according to quantum efficiencies and PEC tests was attributed to 3D scaffold MOF, band gap engineering by Pd plasmons, and light harvesting of S, N GQDs. The turning point of this study is the role of Pd nanoparticles in band-gap engineering. Herein, low cost Pd nanoparticles shown both high LSPR effect and high light absorption. In this work, Pd nanoparticles have approximately sizes of 60-100 nm, which led to high light absorption in the visible region.

Zirconium chloride, 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and methacrylic acid were purchased from the Sigma Aldrich company. Palladium chloride, citric acid, thiourea, styrene monomer, and potassium persulfate were purchased from Merck. Milli-Q (0.05 μS/cm) water and deionized water were used in this work.

Non-cross linked monodisperse polystyrene spheres were fabricated via an emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization technique. In a typical synthesis, a three-necked round-bottom flask equipped with a reflux system containing 100 mL Milli-Q water (0.05 μS/cm) heated before adding styrene monomer (12 mL). Then, methacrylic acid (0.35 g) added under severe stirring and potassium persulfate (0.03 g) as initiator dissolved in water (5 mL) and added to the flask under the purge of N2 gas. The temperature was maintained at 80°C for 24 h. High stable monodispersed polystyrene colloidal crystals (PS) was formed and the size and morphology were depicted in Fig. 1 S (Supporting file). The size distribution curve of PS nanospheres is narrow and PDI (poly-disperse index) is 0.016 which confirmed the monodispersity of the PS nanospheres. Size of PS nanospheres is 250-280 nm and the light was scattered by PS monolayer on the water surface which is related to the self-assembly between PS nanospheres. The 3D-AFM image of the formed monolayer is indicated in insert of Fig. 1S. FTO (2 cm × 1 cm, 22.0 Ω cm−2-Sharif solar company-Iran) was placed for several hours in diluted colloidal crystals for arranging of spherical PS (opal structure) on FTO at 50°C. Prior this step, FTO glasses were sonically cleaned for 15 min in acetone, ethanol, and isopropanol separately. Then, FTO dried under vacuum and used. The stability of the resulting PS opals on the FTO surface was enhanced by post-heating at 90°C for 1 h.

For preparation of UiO-66 film, first, 0.07 g of ZrCl4 dissolved in 2 mL DMF under stirring to obtain a clear solution. Then, 0.05 g BDC linker was also dissolved in 2 mL DMF containing a specific amount of acetic acid. The two solutions were mixed together under stirring for 30 min. In the next step, PS opals on the FTO surface used as substrate for deposition of MOF. Substrate placed on the horizontal surface and it was wet with acetic acid solution. In the next step, 10 μL of UiO-66 sol infiltrated in the template under heat treatment at 120°C. After infiltration of MOF sol, additional solution on the top of the template was whipped by Kim Tech. to hinder thick film creation. At the end, electrode was maintained by 120° C for 3 h and then cooled down to room temperature and finally the electrode was washed with methanol and dried at 70° C for overnight.

For preparation of UiO-66/Pd, certainly amount of PdCl4 was dissolved in 0.1 M chloric acid to reach a clear brown solution. The prepared electrode was dipped in PdCl4 solution under UV (Hg-360 nm) for 30 sec. After withdrawing from solution, the electrode was washed with ethanol and dried under vacuum. White color film changed to light-brown color and confirmed the photodeposition of Pd metal on its surface.

In the first step, S,N GQDs were synthesized with low-cost precursors by the simple hydrothermal method. In brief description, in a vessel, 1 mmol citric acid and 3 mmol thiourea were dissolved in 4 mL DMF solvent. The resulted clear solution transferred to a Teflon container of autoclave and then heated to 180°C and remained for 8 h at this temperature. The final solution was collected by adding absolute ethanol to the resulted solution for thermal treatment and then it was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained solid was easily dispersed in deionized water and the histogram of the nanoparticle diameters and PL spectra for an aqueous solution of S, N GQDs presented in Fig. 2S (Supporting File).

In the second step, Pd/MOF film dipped in S,N GQDs suspension for 5h at room temperature. Then, the electrodes were washed by ethanol several times and dried in a vacuum oven. The light brown color of electrodes was switched to dark brown. The procedure of preparation of photoelectrode was depicted in Schematic 1 with more details.

Phase and crystallinity of photoelectrodes were determined by X-Ray Diffraction (XRD-Explorer GNR- Italia) under a Dectris diffract meter employing Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15418 nm). Shape, elemental and mapping analyses were investigated by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM- MIRA 3 TESCAN) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Surface roughness and morphologies were evaluated by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) (Ara research, Iran). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained by EM, Philips BioTwinCM12. Forthermore, High resolution TEM (TEC9G20-FEI-USA) was also used for characterization of three components of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs. Optical properties of films were evaluated by diffractive reflectance (DRS) UV-Visible spectrophotometer (UV-Vis) using Lab Omed 2950. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for Quantum dots and films were recorded by a Perkin Elmer LS45 spectrofluorophotometer. Solid-phase I-V curves in the dark were plotted from the obtained data of IV-28(IRA-SOL-Iran). The silver paste coating was done on film to make the connection between film and FTO.

The photoelectrochemical (PEC) parameters were evaluated by a three-electrode configuration using an electrochemical instrument SAMA 500 (Iran). The aqueous solution of 0.2 M Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8) as electrolyte was deoxygenated with N2 purging for 30 min prior to measurement. The MOF, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs electrodes were applied as the working electrode with a Pt wire and Ag/AgCl as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. It is worth mentioning that the active surface of photoelectrode for PEC tests was 1 cm2. A 400 W Xe lamp was used as the light source and the scan rates for Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) analysis was 10 mV s−1. Evolved gases were measured by Gas Chromatography (Agilent 7890A-USA) by Ar as a gas carrier. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) in the frequency range of 10-105 Hz was studied by AutoLab (302N-USA). Mott-Schottky measurements were also evaluated by AutoLab in a fixed frequency (100 Hz). The reference potential of Ag/AgCl electrode was converted to the Reactive Hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential according to the Nernst Eq. (1) [39].

The incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) which is equal to the external quantum efficiency were done by using IPCE 020 (IRA SOL-Iran) at a wavelength range of 370-800 nm. Au thin film (≈50 nm in thickness) was coated on photoelectrode as current collector. The IPCE calculated according to Eq. (2) [40].

where λ, I, and Jlight represent the induced light wavelength, photocurrent, and light power density, respectively.

The preparation process of MOF, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs films were depicted in Schematic 1. In the first step, PS nanospheres were arranged on the FTO surface with its dipping in the suspension for a long time. Template-electrode was placed in an oven and then the infiltration of MOF sol was done in-situ on the template. At this step, PS nanospheres get started to dissolve and deforming due to the presence of DMF. But, the presence of acetic acid in the clear MOF sol was controlled the process without any transferring of polymers. Indeed, acetic acid dissolved in DMF and diffuse into DMF/PS solution which led to promote solidification of PS. In the presence of acetic acid, the PS/DMF solution breaks into spherical shapes due to Rayleigh transformation. Dissolved PS in DMF solvent could be dropped due to presence of acetic acid and nanosphers can be formed by precipitation of PS droplets. The diffusion rate of acetic acid in PS/DMF solution could be determined the morphology and size of the formed PS nanospheres [41]. Nevertheless, PS nanospheres are slightly dissolved and leads to more film formation in boundaries in comparison with common inverse opals (template does not dissolve). In the absence of acetic acid, PS nanospheres were completely dissolved and transferring through the FTO surface. After removal of the template, Pd nanoparticles photodeposited on the edge of nano-rings on the inverse opal-like MOF surface and partially deposited inside of nano-rings under UV irradiation. At the end, the MOF/Pd dipped in S,N GQDs suspension for a certain time to form MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs film.

XRD pattern of MOF and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs was shown in Fig. 1. The XRD pattern of MOF including the main peaks at 2θ, 7.0 and 8.3 and these peaks assigned to (111) and (200) crystalline planes, respectively. Crystalline phase of MOF is octahedral according to the data base (CCD, No: 96-153-4786). Furthermore, the pattern for nanocomposite film of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs does not show any additional peaks for Pd or QDs in comparison with MOF film. This could be related to the low amount of deposit Pd nanoparticles on the MOF film and very small size of QDs (<6nm according to Fig. 2 S a).

Shape and appearance of films (PS, MOF, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs) were characterized by FE-SEM microscopy and indicated in Fig. 2. Fig. 2a shows the self-assembly of PS nanospheres on the FTO surface. It was obviously confirmed that PS nanospheres have a homogenous distribution in size (see Fig. 1S, Supporting File). The cross section of self-assembled PS nanospheres was demonstrated in Fig. 2b. The new morphology of inverse opal-like with gyroid-like at the bottom layers of MOF film was shown in Fig. 2c. The main reason for this morphology is related to the film formation process. The PS nanospheres can dissolve in DMF solvent, but in this synthesis procedure, PS did not dissolve in DMF under high temperature (120°C) completely. On the other hand, the presence of acetic acid could be having a high influence in descending of PS dissolving, hampered of moving and transferring of dissolved polymer. In addition, the contact time of DMF and PS nanospheres was short due to in-situ infiltration under heating treatment. These structures have some advantages such as having a variety of pores with different sizes that can be seen by cross section SEM profile in Fig. 2d. Fig. 2e was clearly shown the deposited Pd nanoparticles in edges of inverse opal structures. Fig. 2f was also depicted the decorating of Pd nanoparticles and QDs thin layer on the MOF surface. Furthermore, it was obviously observed that all surfaces including pores was coated with quantum dots. It seems that a very thin layer covered all film surfaces. The color of Film (inset Fig. 2f) was changed to dark brown in comparison with MOF films (inset Fig. 2c).

Elemental analysis was evaluated by EDX and spectra demonstrated in Fig. 3 S (Supporting file). The EDX spectra for MOF indicated the emergence of Zr, O and C peaks that can be attributed to UiO-66 compound. After deposition of Pd nanoparticles on the MOF surface, a weak peak for Pd element also appeared in the EDS spectrum. At the end, the decoration of quantum dots on the MOF/Pd led to appearance of S and N peaks in the EDX spectrum. It is noticeable that C peak in the EDX spectra of MOF and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs has different intensities which could be related to graphene in quantum dots. In EDS spectra, there is a Sn peak, which can be indexed to substrate (FTO). Mapping analyses for selected area of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs were depicted in Fig. 4 S (Supporting file) and confirmed the presence of Zr, O, C, Pd, S and N elements. Mapping and EDS analyses are suggesting that the proposed method was successful for new-design of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrode.
In elemental mapping, color intensity of Pd is very low which is related to the low amount of Pd loaded on the MOF surface.

The morphology of the films was also evaluated by AFM technique and the captured images were inserted in Fig. 3. Fig. 3a demonstrates the new morphology of inverse opal-like of MOF from top view. Fig. 3b also presents the 3D-AFM image of MOF films from top view that clearly indicates the growth of the MOF nanorigns with special arrangement. The film thickness is 1005 nm and the 3D image also indicates the formation of nanorings that have walls with special heights like nanobowles. The deposition of Pd plasmonic nanoparticles and S,N GQDs on the MOF film were depicted in Fig. 3c. TEM and HRTEM images of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs were also depicted in Fig. 5 S (Supporting file). As seen in Fig. 5Sa, the inverse opal structures for MOF can be observed and the dark points were attributed to Pd nanoparticles and S,N GQDs. HRTEM image in Fig. 5Sb clearly indicated Pd nanoparticles, which grown on the MOF surface. Furthermore, S,N GQDs covered both surface deposited Pd nanoparticles and MOF.

DRS UV-Vis spectra of the MOF, MOF/Pd, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs films were investigated in order to evaluate the optical and light absorption performance of photoelectrodes. As shown in Fig. 4a, for inverse opal-like MOF, the characteristic peak of UiO-66 in wavelength 275 nm was attributed to UV active-feature of MOF based on previous reports [42]. Furthermore, the edge absorption of UiO-66 at 338 nm could be indexed to the π - π* transition in linkers [43]. Cui et all [44] shown semiconducting feature of NiFe-MOF for effective heterojunction of NiFe-MOF/TiO2 photoelectrodes. They indicated that NiFe-MOF could be acted as active sites for light harvesting. For MOF/S,N GQDs, the absorption of the compound in visible region was increased which indexed to the intrinsic ability of QDs in light sorption. For the MOF/Pd film, it shows the high effective role of Pd nanoparticles in transferring of absorption edge to higher wavelengths and enhancing surface area of absorption of visible light. Despite the poor performance of plasmonic effects palladium, in this work, it can be highlighted the Pd plasmonic properties and production of hot electrons which transport to conduction band (CB) of MOF. For MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs film, both absorption edge and light harvesting in visible region are increasing. Pd and QDs have synergistic effects in activation of UiO-66 inverse opals in the visible region. Cui et all [45] were also reported higher light adsorption by NH2-MIL-125/TiO2 photoanode decorated with Ag plasmonic metal due to light trapping effect of plasmonic metal. Furthermore, S,N GQDs can be acted as a protective layer for Pd nanoparticles in water solution. The band gap of MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs films was calculated and presented in Fig. 4b. According to this Figure, the band gap energies are 3.95, 3.74, 3.21, and 2.96 eV for MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs, respectively.

One of the most important parameters in photocatalytic process is light harvesting ability of photocatalyst materials. Complicated structures such as inverse-opal like or specific 3D structure has key role in light trapping and harvesting. Herein, the light harvesting efficiency (LHE) calculated from UV-Vis absorption data by Eq. 4 [46]:
(4)
LHE = 1 − 1
0
−
A (
λ )
where, A(λ) is absorbance at a wavelength (λ).

It is noteworthy that photocatalytic activity has direct relation with the percent of LHE. As can be seen in Fig. 6 Sa (Supporting file), LHE in the visible region at 500 nm for MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs is 50, 65, 74, 82%, respectively. Inverse opal-like MOF has a high LHE due to its specific 3D structure that has a main role in trapping and multi-scattering of light. On the other hand, inverse opal-like structure could have high efficiency of light harvesting due to slow photon phenomena [47]. MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs was also shown the highest light harvesting due to the synergistic effect of Pd and QDs in light harvesting in addition of inverse opal-like structure. The PL studies are often applied for evaluating the fate of photogenerated hole-electron pairs on the photocatalyst surface. Fig. 6Sb (Supporting file) depicts PL emission of MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs films under excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It was clearly observed that the MOF film and the MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs film have the highest and lowest emissions, respectively. Low emission of PL represents the low recombination rate or high separation of hole-electron pairs. About triplet nanocomposite film, the lowest recombination rate is due to two reasons. One is related to the simultaneous presence of Pd and QDs and another can be attributed to the inverse opal-like structure of MOF [48]. Both of Pd and QDs have an effective role in decreasing PL emission. Comparison between MOF/S,N GQDs and MOF/Pd films clarifies that Pd nanaoparticles are more effective than S,N GQDs in descending of recombination phenomena. This could be attributed to the injection of hot electrons produced from plasmonic properties of Pd nanoparticles to the conduction band (CB) of MOF and enhanced separation of the charge carriers [23]. But S,N GQDs have more influence in light absorption than Pd nanoparticles. However, junction of MOF with Pd and S,N GQDs was led to decrease the recombination rate to about 3.5 times in comparison with MOF alone.

The PEC performance was investigated on the MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs samples (Fig. 5). Open circuit potential (OCP) test was demonstrated for these samples in Fig. 5a. OCP for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs electrode was more negative than other samples which explained that this modified electrode has the highest potential for water oxidation process compared to other photoelectrodes. The external onset potential for triplet composite was exceeded about 0.48V, 0.28 V and 0.16 V compared to MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs and MOF/Pd, respectively. In OCP analysis, the slop of curves is related to the lifetime of the photo generated charge carriers or their recombination rate. As it is observed, MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs has the lowest slope in comparison with other electrodes. Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) is an effective method for examining the charge separation in the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface. The I-V curves show a significant enhancement in photocurrent density after MOF was decorated by QDs and Pd plasmonic nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 5b, the inverse opal-like MOF displays a photocurrent density of ≈10 μA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs RHE. Low photocurrent of MOF film could be attributed to the high charge transfer resistance of MOFs under bias potentials. The photocurrent density of the MOF/ S,N GQDs increased to 45 μA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which was 4.5 times greater than that of the MOF film. In nanocomposite MOF/Pd, the photocurrent reached to 95 μA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE which was 2 times greater than MOF/S,N GQDs composite. Guan et all [49] have shown the modification of BiVO4 photoanode by using anchors Ag metals due to higher charge separation and SPR effect. In nanocomposite MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs, the photocurrent is about 297 μA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE which is 29.7, 6.6 and 3.12 times greater than MOF, MOF/ S,N GQDs and MOF/Pd films, respectively. It could be attributed to the higher separation or lower recombination rates of electron and hole. According to the results obtained, Pd was more effective than QDs in the photocurrent density. Fig. 5c shows the chopped illumination of photoelectrodes and the current is switched by successive On and Off state of irradiation source. Furthermore, the photo-stability of electrodes were evaluated by long time use of them for PEC water splitting at AM 1.5 (100 mW/cm2). As shown in Fig. 5d, all of them almost has high photo-stability for 3000 sec. It can also be noticed that the new-design of photoelectrodes shown high potential for using in future applicable technologies.

Chronoamperometric test indicates the sensitivity of the photoelectrodes to visible light and the results are depicted in Fig. 6a. Transient current for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs nanocomposite film is higher than other films which is attributed to the synergistic effect of Pd and QDs. Pd nanoparticles produce hot electrons under light irradiation due to its plasmonic feature. The transfer of hot electrons to the conduction band of MOF significantly reduces charge transfer resistance of MOF film. To show MOF effect, all of PEC tests were done for Pd, S,N GQDs, and Pd/S,N GQDs under light illumination. Results were indicated in Fig. 7 S that prove Pd, S,N GQDs and Pd/S,N GQDs have low PEC efficiencies compered to MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs. These results suggest that the MOF plays a key role in PEC performance.

To identify interfacial properties between photoelectrode and electrolyte, EIS technique was done at frequency range 10−1-105 Hz. Fig. 6b depicts the EIS spectra related to MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs, MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs under AM 1.5 (100 mW/cm−2). Arc radius represents the charge transfer resistance of films in Nyquist curves. It clearly presents that the MOF film has high charge resistance due to its intrinsic resistance under bias potential. MOF/S,N GQDs has lower resistance than MOF, it could be referred to S,N GQDs in light absorption and effective heterojunction with MOF. MOF film loaded Pd nanoparticles demonstrates lower resistance than both MOF and MOF/S,N GQDs films which are related to the plasmonic effect of Pd. The very low resistance of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs could be attributed to the synergistic effect of Pd and QDs in charge transfer. Resistance versus charge transferring of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs is about one-sixth, one-fifth and half times of MOF, MOF/S,N GQDs and MOF/Pd, respectively. To determine the electronic features such as charge carrier density (Nd) and flat band potential (Vfb) of photo-electrodes, the AC impedance for samples was investigated in the dark. The highest photocurrent was also assigned to the high production of charge density (Nd) which calculated from Mott-Schottky (M-S) plot by Eq. 5 [39,40].

where, e, ε and ε0 represents the electron charge, dielectric constant of the material, and vacuum permittivity, respectively. C and v are film capacity and applied potential, respectively. Positive slope of the M-S curve for MOF represents the n-type of synthesized UiO-66. It should be noted that the Nd is inversely proportional to the slope of M-S curves. As indicated in Fig. 6c, the slope of M-S decreased for MOF, MOF/Pd, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrodes, accordingly. The lowest slope of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs represents the highest production of charges in interfacial photoelectrode and electrolyte. The flat band potential (Vfb) of the photoelectrodes can be obtained by extrapolating the slopes of M-S plot to intercept the x axes. The most negative shift of flat band potential for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs (-0.64 V) was strongly represented the highest potential of photoelectrode for water oxidation. Band positions of the three samples were calculated and shown in Fig. 3S (Supporting File). Position of the valence band (VB) for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrode indicated a high potential of film for oxidation of water molecules. The lifetime (τn) of photogenerated electrons in different photoelectrodes inversely proportion to the frequency at peaks (fmax) in Bode plots according to Eq. 6 [50].

2 π
f
m a x
)

Based on Fig. 6d, the Bode phase plots of the three different photoelectrodes show the smallest fmax for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs in comparison with MOF/Pd and MOF. This behavior suggests a longer lifetime of electrons and lowest recombination rate for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrode. As inset in Fig. 6d depicts, the life time of electrons are 0.599, 0.765, 0.829, and 1.443 ms for MOF, MOF/Pd, MOF/S,N GQDs, and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs, respectively. Photogenerated electrons of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs have lifetime almost 2.4 and 1.7 times greater than MOF, and MOF/Pd, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 7a, the optimal ABPE was obtained 0.66% at 0.65 V vs RHE for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrode. ABPE was also determined for MOF/Pd, MOF/S,N GQDs, and MOF at efficiencies of 0.33%, 0.15%, and 0.04% under the same conditions, respectively. It clearly observed that MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs has the highest efficiency, which related to the synergistic effect of Pd and QDs and proper combination of them on the MOF surface. External quantum efficiency (EQE) stated by the incident photon-to-electron efficiency (IPCE). The remarkable increase of efficiency for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs in the wavelength range of 370-800 nm represents in Fig. 7b. The enhancement of efficiency for MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs might be arising from high charge separation and light harvesting. IPCE curve was clearly proved that the modification of MOF with Pd and S,N GQDs leads to higher light absorption in the visible light region. To more elucidation of the morphology effect (inverse opal like MOF), absorbed photon to electron conversion efficiency (APCE) was investigated. The APCE as an internal quantum efficiency is calculated by dividing of IPCE to LHE at each wavelength by Eq. 7 [46].
(7)
APCE =
IPCE / LHE

The proper results from APCE was confirmed the participation of inverse opal like structures in charge migration in PEC water splitting. Based on the APCE plot in Fig. 7c, MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs has the highest efficiency in production of photocurrent which is related to more routes for charge migration. The solid state I-V curves were also inserted in Fig. 7d and the linear relation was obtained for all photoelectrodes. The linear relations confirm the presence of proper ohmic contacts between films and the substrate. MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs film is shown the lowest charge resistance due to provide multi canals for transporting charges.

To confirm the photocurrent originated from the water splitting process, determination of evolved gases is crucial. The evolved gases around of photoelectrode and Pt electrode measured by GC analysis. Results in Fig. 8
a,c, show the determination of evolved H2 and O2 gases around two photoelectrode MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs. Photocurrent corresponding to two photoelectrodes was evaluated under light irradiation for calculating the theoretical gas production rates, which can see in Fig. 8 b,d. Three measurments and their error bars were confirmed the reproducibility of evolved gases in these systems. Right axes in Fig. 8 a,c depict the faradic efficiencies for MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs and attributes to the ratio of real/theory evolved gases. Faradic efficiency for MOF/Pd and MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs are above 94%. High faradic efficiency for both photoelectrodes was assigned to the fact that the obtained photocurrents originated from water splitting. The molar ratio of H2 to O2 gases is 1.86:1 and the deviation from stoichiometry ratio (2:1) was probably originated from manual sampling for GC analysis. XRD pattern and SEM image of prepared photoelectrode after PEC test indicated in Fig. 4S a and Fig. 4S b (Supporting File), respectively which confirmed the high stability of the photoelectrdes.

Proposed mechanism for the PEC performance of MOF/Pd/S,N GQDs photoelectrode was graphically depicted in Schematic 2 . Generally, under light irradiation, wrapped thin layer of QDs acted as absorber of broaden visible light region due to S and N co-doped to graphene quantum dots [38]. Furthermore, QDs can produce charge pairs under visible light irradiation due to its narrow band gap. Pd nanoparticles could produce hot electrons due to surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) feature which these electrons transferring to the conduction band (CB) of UiO-66 and transporting in inverse opal-like structure to reach the substrate. Beside charge transporting in the complex structure of MOF[17], inverse opal-like structure also provided variety routes for electron transporting. Photogenerated holes obtained from the MOF and QDs on the films can oxidize water molecules. Herein, QDs play two roles both as a light absorber and as a co-catalyst. Furthermore, QDs could be prevented oxidizing of Pd nanoparticles. Photogenerated electrons were also moved to background and transferring to counter electrode for reducing water. Morphology of UiO-66 film also has highlighted role in light scattering and harvesting. Formed mesoporous and microporous in this structure suggested sites for light multi-scattering. On the other hand, photogenerated electrons act as major charges that move to background and transferring to counter electrode (Pt) for water reduction.

Compative study of prepared film of this work with some other MOF-films show in Table 1 . It is well known that, MOFs were often used as photosensitizer or co-catalyst and less used as catalyst due to its boarding band gap and its resistance. On the other hand, MOFs show high electrocatalytic activity for water splitting and this is a motivation for using in the photo-electrocatalytic process.

Studies conducted on the photoelectrocatalytic activity of UiO-66 (MOF) as high stable MOF [51] are very low, as shown in Table 1. UiO-66/Pd/S,N GQDs introduces as a modified electrode for PEC water splitting which used on MOF's semiconducting property. About UiO-66/Pd[43], photoelectrochemical response is very low which is attributed to morphology and Pd effects in composite. Co and Ni compounds play main role in the photocatalytic activity of composites such as Co2(bim)4/BiVO4, Fe2O3/Co/MIm, InNi-MOF/TiO2, or CoNi-MOFs/BiVO4 in water splitting. In this work, for the first time, a high stable UiO-66 with new morphology was used as photoelectrode material with proper semiconducting property.

To sum, for the first time, new morphology of 3D inverse opal-like UiO-66 films were synthesized by infiltration of UiO-66 sol in the colloidal PS template. Resultant 3D inverse opal-like UiO-66 films systematically decorated by Pd nanoparticles and S,N GQDs as a new design of photoelectrode for PEC water splitting. It can be highlighted that Pd nanoparticles have two effective roles in band engineering due to its light absorption and surface plasmonic resonance properties. An innovative morphology of UiO-66, band gap engineering by Pd plasmons, and light harvesting of QDs were contributed in producing of high photocurrent according to LSV and EIS analyses. This enhancement was attributed to effective photo-induced charge transporting and low recombination due to complicated 3D ordered MOF that produces some routes for charge transporting. Pd and QDs simultaneously could affect the band gap reduction and light absorption edge. High internal quantum efficiency ≈ 20% at 500 nm confirmed that the new morphology has a key role in production of photocurrent due to light harvesting and provide transporting routes for charge transferring. ABPE of 0.66% at 0.65 V vs RHE also indicates a successful incorporation of Pd and QDs on MOF surface. This strategy is helpful for creating of new effective design for MOF thin films despite their intrinsic high resistance.

Mahboobeh Zargazi: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Mohammad H. Entezari: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
